08 Subjectivity & Automation - YouTube.mp4
One of the things you'll notice about our scorecard system is that it is both manual and subjective. In other words, the data itself needs to be input manually each week. And the color coding is set by the person who owns that metric, not by some fancy formula or algorithm. Let me be clear. This is a feature, not a... a bug. In other words, both the manual and subjective elements of this scorecarding system are intentional. Now I am blessed to have a really smart team. They know how to configure APIs and write formulas that could automate literally every aspect of the company scorecard system that I just showed you. In fact, on more than one occasion, we've invested...
many tens of thousands of dollars to do just that, only to abandon the supposedly more efficient, more accurate system for the slow manual process that I just showed you. So why, right? Why is manual and subjective superior to automated and objective? There are two reasons. Greater insights, and greater ownership. First, when your team is forced to interact with the numbers themselves, they begin to truly know their numbers. They develop an almost sixth sense where they can feel when the numbers seem off. Said another way, their insights about the data skyrockets. If you've seen the movie The Matrix, you probably remember the scene where Neo first saw the Matrix. I mean, at first, all he noticed were the long lines of ones and zeros, but as Neo's knowledge and skills improved, those ones and zeros transformed into to vivid pictures.
And by the end, he could literally bend the matrix to his will. While not quite that extreme, that general idea will become a reality for your team. With the data each and every week, they will see pictures, patterns, and themes where everyone else just sees numbers. Contrast this with a purely automated scorecard. That pulls in numbers and spits out pretty colors and graphs, you know, sure, these will look nice, but over time, your people will become divorced from what the actual numbers mean, and they lose the ability to add insights and commentary. Oh yeah, and when something breaks— which only always happens by the way— it can sometimes take weeks to notice the error, because no one on your team has a sense of what the numbers should be.
This is why manual scorecards provide greater insights than automated scorecards. The second reason we prefer the manual and subjective option is because it creates a greater sense of ownership. When a formula says that a given metric is yellow or green for a few weeks only to have it finish in the red, it's easy to blame the formula. But when you're forced to look at the actual and compare it to the target, knowing everything that you know, all of a sudden the line between orange, yellow, and light green becomes very, very thin. And that is exactly the point. When you aren't sure, when your team isn't sure, you're going to dig a little deeper. You're going to want to learn more because you're going to want to get to the right answer.
And when you miss it, you're going to. it. It's going to sting, and you're going to strive to do better the next time. Also, when you or your team is walking into a meeting knowing that there's a lot of red, orange, and yellow in their category, you better believe that they're gonna show up with ideas and suggestions rather than just being surprised at the end of the month when the goals just aren't hitting. They just aren't sure what's working, right? So resist the urge to build automated scorecards. Yes, it can be done. Obviously, it can be done, but the experience and insights your team will gain. By entering their data manually and then subjectively selecting their color codes, will more than offset any time they might have saved by tasking a computer to do the dirty work of scale.
